text: I like cartographic over spatial because apple has overloaded the term, recently.

elements.text: I like cartographic over spatial because apple has overloaded the term, recently

text: <https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9320477|https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9320477> used diagrammatic programming as a subgenre of visual programming

elements.text: used diagrammatic programming as a subgenre of visual programming

text:
"spatial" is becoming very overloaded recently, with apple adopting it for their VR stuff. I use it to specify that it's "not sentential programming". I took it from SPLAT, which stands for Spatial Programming Language with Ascii Text.

but i misused it - I took the wrong part. the thing that makes it "non-sentential" is the ASCII TEXT, not the SPATIAL PROGRAMMING. it compiles to Ulam, which also works with spatial data, but using a sentential syntax. 

essentially I don't care what terminology i use for it. the key thing is that I show how to program by arranging before-and-after diagrams

but i misused it - I took the wrong part. the thing that makes it "non-sentential" is the ASCII TEXT, not the SPATIAL PROGRAMMING. it compiles to Ulam, which also works with spatial data, but using a sentential syntax. 

essentially I don't care what terminology i use for it. the key thing is that I show how to program by arranging before-and-after diagrams

text:
Spatial programming definitely goes way back. I've mostly seen it used in a similar sense to how Apple are using it, so it doesn't bother me too much. (But I haven't travelled much in the automata circles)

The term I like is "graphical programming", but I've also learned that this term has some historical usage I don't agree with.

So I'm kinda with Lu. It's like trying to describe musical genre. I don't mind doing it for other music, but for my own music? No. Never.

I'm just going to make the things that interest me and let others figure out what to call them.

(I've been told I make prog rock, which feels about as mortally offensive as claiming "node-wire" is a programming paradigm)

text: Oh I really like "cartographic programming". Maps are all about integrating continuous space with discrete markers, and textual labels and they work really well. The metaphor also works for me in terms of the relationship between code and what it represents, being like the relationship between a map and territory.
